Green hydrogen for a sustainable future: A review of production methods, innovations, and applications
======================================================================================================

Highlights
----------

*   •This article traces about the pathway of Green Hydrogen & history of Hydrogen. 
*   •How Hydrogen is going to decarbonize the fossil fuel based world in sustainable way. 
*   •A brief explanation about types of Hydrogen based on production method. 
*   •Exclusively about Carbon neutral Green Hydrogen and its production method-water electrolysis. 
*   •How seawater and wastewater acts as a potential source for Green Hydrogen production. 

Abstract
--------

Green hydrogen is essential for advancing sustainable energy systems and achieving a low-carbon economy. Various production methods have been developed, each with distinct technological characteristics, efficiency levels, and environmental impacts. Conventional and advanced approaches include water electrolysis such as alkaline electrolysis, proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysis, and solid oxide electrolysis (SOE)—as well as innovative processes like biological hydrogen production through biophotolysis, fermentation, biological water-gas shift reactions, and microbial fuel cells. Other significant techniques encompass thermochemical hydrogen production methods like biomass gasification, steam methane reforming, and thermochemical cycles, alongside photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting systems, including single-junction, dual-junction, integrated PEC cells, and photocatalytic PEC cells. Emerging plasma-assisted water splitting methods, such as corona discharge, dielectric barrier discharge (DBD), and microwave plasma-assisted splitting, also offer promising alternatives for efficient hydrogen production. Among these methods, water electrolysis powered by renewable electricity holds substantial potential for large-scale green hydrogen production. Photoelectrochemical water splitting and plasma-assisted methods have demonstrated unique advantages in terms of sustainability and integration with renewable energy sources. The scalability, cost-effectiveness, and environmental benefits of these processes depend on technological advancements in reactor design, materials, and catalytic efficiency. Integrating green hydrogen with carbon sequestration technologies further enhances its role in reducing carbon footprints and promoting sustainability. Sustainable applications extend to energy storage solutions, where green hydrogen can store surplus renewable energy, and transportation, particularly in fuel cell vehicles, promoting zero-emission mobility. This review examines these hydrogen production methods, highlighting their efficiencies, challenges, and recent innovations that are pivotal for achieving competitive, large-scale green hydrogen production. Furthermore, the integration of green hydrogen with carbon sequestration technologies is discussed, underscoring its role in reducing carbon footprints and enhancing sustainability.

Introduction
------------

Hydrogen, the lightest and most plentiful element in the universe, has emerged as a cornerstone in the global transition to sustainable power solutions. As a zero-emission fuel that produces only water when combusted, hydrogen has captivated worldwide attention for its potential to power a sustainable and greener future. With the depletion of crude oil reserves and the pressing need to mitigate climate change, scientific and industrial efforts are increasingly focused on renewable energy solutions. These efforts align with international agreements such as the Montreal Protocol (1987), Kyoto Protocol (1997), and Paris Agreement (2015), which emphasize the reduction of emissions of greenhouse gas and the adoption of sustainable practices [[1], [2], [3], [4]].

Hydrogen stands out for its unparalleled energy storage capacity—three times that of gasoline and seven times that of coal—making it an attractive alternative for addressing the energy demands of a growing global population. Despite fossil fuels dominating the energy market with a 70% share in 2023 and contributing to an annual release of 36.8 billion metric tons of CO 2, renewable energy transition is gaining momentum globally [5]. Solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, and biomass energy offer cleaner, more sustainable alternatives, though they face challenges like intermittency, unpredictability, and high storage costs [6]. Amid these limitations, green hydrogen, formed through water electrolysis powered by renewable electricity, has manifested as a transformative solution. It not only stores renewable energy for consistent availability but also plays a pivotal role in attaining Sustainable Development Goal 7—ensuring access to affordable, reliable, and sustainable energy [7].

While the promise of hydrogen as a clean, sustainable fuel is significant, several challenges must be addressed. Its low density, high flammability, and the complexities associated with storage and transportation pose hurdles to its widespread adoption [8,9]. Nevertheless, hydrogen's potential to decarbonize transportation, heavy industry, and power generation aligns seamlessly with global sustainability goals, making it a key player to combat climate change [10].

As hydrogen's role in the global energy mix expands, innovations in its production, storage, and distribution are critical. The development of cost-effective technologies, such as electrolysis powered by renewable energy, and the establishment of supportive infrastructure and regulatory frameworks, will be key to harnessing the full potential of the hydrogen economy by 2050. This article delves into the innovations driving green hydrogen production and examines its transformative role in shaping a sustainable future [11].

Hydrogen production methods are broadly categorized into two main approaches: reforming fossil fuels like methane and methanol, and splitting water through electrolysis [12]. Fossil fuel reforming, including steam methane reforming (SMR) and coal gasification, dominates global hydrogen production, accounting for approximately 76% of output. However, these methods are unsustainable and carbon-intensive, emitting 8–30 kg of CO 2 per kg of hydrogen produced [13]. While SMR is an endothermic and efficient process, it relies on finite natural gas reserves and has a significant impact on greenhouse gas emissions. To address these challenges, the focus must shift toward carbon-free sources such as water electrolysis, biomass gasification, and metal-water splitting.

Water, as the most promising hydrogen-rich source, offers a 2:1 hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio, and electrolysis generates no direct emissions. Biomass gasification utilizes organic material with minimal carbon footprint, while metal-water splitting employs reactive metals to facilitate hydrogen production, though its dependence on mining raises sustainability concerns [14]. Transitioning to these carbon-free methods, particularly electrolytic production of green hydrogen using renewable energy, offers a sustainable path to carbon-neutral hydrogen production, aligning with global climate goals [15,16].

Green hydrogen derived from water electrolysis using renewable power, is a highly promising energy carrier due to its unique properties. Physically, the molecular weight of hydrogen is 2.016 g/mol, making it the lightest element, and exists as a colorless, odorless, and tasteless gas. It has an extremely low density of 0.08988 kg/m 3 at standard temperature and pressure (STP), making it about 14 times lighter than air [17]. Chemically, hydrogen is highly reactive, possessing the highest energy content per unit mass among fuels at 120 MJ/kg. Its combustion produces only water, ensuring zero direct carbon emissions, which positions it as a clean alternative to fossil fuels [18]. Environmentally, green hydrogen is sustainable and produced using renewable energy, substantially cutting reliance on non-renewable resources and greenhouse gas emissions [19]. Green hydrogen production made more efficient by combining hybrid systems such as integrating renewable energy sources like solar, wind and hydro with electrolysis [7].

However, its volumetric energy density is low, necessitating advanced storage solutions such as high-pressure tanks, liquefaction, or metal hydrides. Furthermore, hydrogen's versatility allows its use in diverse applications, including energy storage, transportation, and industrial processes, as well as its function as a precursor for synthetic fuels [16]. These properties highlight the potential of green hydrogen as a cornerstone of the global transition to a sustainable and decarbonized energy future.

# Important Design Considerations for Building Green Hydrogen Infrastructure

Developing robust green hydrogen infrastructure is critical in transitioning to a low-carbon economy.

![A green hydrogen renewable energy pipeline.](https://img.designdevelopmenttoday.com/files/base/indm/multi/image/2024/10/iStock_1404665457.670d8ae648ec8.png?auto=format%2Ccompress&q=70&rect=0%2C151%2C3855%2C2168&w=400)

As the world progressively pivots toward sustainable energy, developing robust green hydrogen infrastructure is critical in transitioning to a low-carbon economy. For industry stakeholders, the challenge extends beyond producing fuel from renewable resources. Successful deployment requires a nuanced understanding of various design considerations to balance technological advancement with scalability and environmental stewardship.

### 3 Most Critical Green Hydrogen Infrastructure

Building a resilient H2 framework means accounting for the complete process from production to the point of end-use. This approach encompasses three primary aspects — advanced production technologies, innovative storage solutions and resilient distribution networks.

Together, these systems can [create a highly viable alternative fuel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0360319923045056#:~:text=When%20hydrogen%20is%20produced%20using,and%20the%20resulting%20climate%20change.) capable of overcoming the limitations of other renewables, particularly in notoriously difficult-to-abate sectors like transportation and construction. 

### 1. Design Considerations in Green Hydrogen Production

Generating green H2 primarily hinges on leveraging renewable energy sources to [split water into hydrogen and oxygen](https://www.meco.com/how-is-green-hydrogen-produced/) and then capture the gas output. The following are the most significant factors for designing a sustainable and efficient electrolysis process. 

### Energy Source Integration

Green hydrogen production faces challenges due to the energy intensity of electrolysis. This poses two challenges — reducing the carbon footprint associated with electricity generation and improving production technology efficiency. Advancements in renewable energy systems, such as next-gen wind turbines and solar panels, coupled with grid modernization, are vital to providing clean power to electrolyzers. 

This concern was the highlight of the recently concluded 5th European Energy Investment Forum in Uruguay. EU Commissioner Kadri Simson emphasized the abundance of Uruguay’s wind and solar resources as a practical source for powering green hydrogen production. She also [announced €2 million in funding](https://energynews.pro/en/commissioner-simson-highlights-the-importance-of-green-hydrogen-in-the-energy-transition-between-the-eu-and-uruguay/) to support the development of relevant infrastructure and facilitate a clean transition. 

### Electrolyzer Technology Selection

For green H2 to be economically viable, its production costs and efficiency must be comparable to those of other fuels. This consideration mainly comes down to the technology used in the process. Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) electrolyzers are an increasingly popular option, but they have an [average capital cost of $2,000](https://www.hydrogen.energy.gov/docs/hydrogenprogramlibraries/pdfs/24005-clean-hydrogen-production-cost-pem-electrolyzer.pdf?sfvrsn=8cb10889_1) per kW of installed capacity. 

Alkaline electrolysis is an older, more established hydrogen production process that uses liquid electrolytes to facilitate the electrochemical reaction. Though less efficient than PEM, this method offers lower capital costs, [ranging from $800-$1,500](https://www.oxfordenergy.org/wpcms/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/Cost-competitive-green-hydrogen-how-to-lower-the-cost-of-electrolysers-EL47.pdf) per kW.

Photoelectrochemical water splitting is another option. It uses sunlight to drive the water electrolysis through semiconductor materials directly. This technology [combines solar power generation and hydrogen production](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/hydrogen-production-photoelectrochemical-water-splitting#:~:text=In%20photoelectrochemical%20(PEC)%20water%20splitting,molecules%20into%20hydrogen%20and%20oxygen.) in a single process, potentially simplifying the infrastructure needed. However, it is not mainstream and not yet a practical choice for wide-scale deployment.

### 2. Green H2 Storage Considerations

Innovative storage solutions represent the second critical pillar for building reliable green hydrogen infrastructure. Several key design factors influence the effectiveness and sustainability of these systems.

### Safety and Efficiency

Hydrogen is generally non-toxic because it is lighter than air and rapidly dissipates when released into the atmosphere. Nevertheless, some of its properties require reinforced engineering controls to enable its safe storage and use. For example, H2 is flammable and can [corrode some metals more quickly](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266726692200010X#:~:text=Hydrogen%20affects%20the%20electrochemical%20kinetics,the%20rate%20of%20intergranular%20corrosion.&text=The%20interaction%20of%20hydrogen%20and,especially%20in%20the%20welded%20joints.) than others, leading to embrittlement. 

The selection of storage materials must also account for potential fuel degradation. Maintaining the purity of stored hydrogen is essential to avoid contamination risks, especially for long-term applications. 

### Capacity and Scalability

It is vital to design storage systems with adequate capacity to meet current demand while allowing for future expansion. Scalability ensures that storage solutions can adapt to changing requirements and accommodate increased hydrogen production or usage over time.

### Environmental Impact

Considering the environmental impact of storage solutions is vital, given that green H2 emerged from the need for sustainable, cleaner fuels. Stakeholders must assess crucial factors, such as energy consumption, emissions and resource usage in repository facilities to ensure they have minimal carbon footprint. 

### Reliability

Ensuring the reliability and resilience of storage systems is essential for maintaining a continuous and stable hydrogen supply. Designing solutions that can withstand potential disruptions, such as power outages or equipment failures, helps guarantee a reliable hydrogen source for various applications.

### 3. Distribution Infrastructure Design

Developing an efficient green hydrogen distribution network is crucial for ensuring seamless delivery to end users. The most important considerations include:

### Proximity to Production and Storage Facilities

Seamlessly integrating transportation systems with production and storage infrastructure ensures a continuous and reliable H2 supply while balancing fluctuations in demand. 

Discussions on the best delivery methods center around the distribution distance. For example, a large, centrally located production facility can produce higher hydrogen volumes but costs more to distribute to farther point-of-use locations. Similarly, dispersed establishments can lower delivery costs but make production more expensive due to fewer total generated volumes.

### Transportation Modes

It is essential to select the most suitable distribution modes based on factors like distance, volume, and cost considerations. Due to its lightweight nature, hydrogen must be [liquefied or pressurized into compressed gas](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/hydrogen-delivery#:~:text=Infrastructure%20includes%20the%20pipelines%2C%20liquefaction,the%20process%20of%20delivering%20fuel.) for transportation. Current delivery methods include cryogenic tanks, gas tube trailers or existing fuel pipelines. 

However, [experts have raised concerns](https://www.ecoticias.com/en/pipelines-hydrogen-blending/6648/) about relying on oil and gas pipelines to transport hydrogen, citing potential issues related to leakage at seals and valve points. They also expressed worry regarding the added stress on the infrastructure, which could lead to expensive damage. 

### Regulatory Compliance

As green H2 adoption improves, adhering to regulatory requirements and industry standards governing distribution is paramount, especially for cross-border deliveries. Staying up to date with evolving regulations helps maintain the transportation network’s integrity and fosters trust among stakeholders.

### Green H2 Market Outlook

The green hydrogen sector is poised for significant growth as industries and governments worldwide shift toward sustainable energy solutions. Key factors shaping the market outlook include:

End-use applications: Green hydrogen finds applications across various sectors, including transportation, industrial processes, and energy storage. Designing systems tailored to these sectors' specific needs is crucial for driving adoption.

Market analysis: Assessing the potential markets for hydrogen is essential for aligning production and distribution systems to meet specific demands. Integrating existing infrastructures and technologies is key to ensuring a smooth transition.

Fuel cell integration: With a projected value of [$130 billion by 2030](https://revolutionized.com/hydrogen-fuel-cell-trucks/), ensuring compatibility with fuel cell technologies is vital for expanding the market reach of green hydrogen. 

Decarbonization potential: Evaluating how hydrogen can replace fossil fuels in various sectors is key to maximizing environmental benefits and accelerating widespread adoption. 

### Embracing Reliable Green Hydrogen Infrastructure

The various design considerations for green H2 systems underscore the value of strategic planning and innovation in fostering a sustainable transition to a cleaner energy future. By carefully addressing the key factors of production, storage, and distribution, stakeholders can build a solid foundation for its extensive application.

INTRODUCTION The opportunities for generating carbon-free hydrogen using nuclear power are significant [1] [2]. For the past decade, NuScale Power has studied multiple hydrogen technologies and integration pathways and remains actively engaged in the development of hydrogen technologies with major industrial partners and national laboratories. A NuScale Nuclear Power Module™ (NPM) produces sufficient power to generate 45 MT/day. A 6-module plant can produce 268 MT/day and 12-module plant 536 MT/day via Electrolysis. NuScale major investors and strategic supply chain partners with hydrogen production, hydrogen storage and hydrogen system integration capabilities include Doosan Enerbility, Samsung, JGC and IHI. These companies have deployed hydrogen production or storage facilities globally. This white paper summarizes NuScale efforts on integrating a NuScale Power Module™ (NPM) with hydrogen production systems. Some key advantages of using a NuScale multi-module system for process hydrogen generation are: • Industrial scale production of Steam, Electric Power and Hydrogen. • Compatible with Electrolyzer technologies and with pathways using Natural Gas • Steam Temperature and Pressure augmentation for higher temperature/ pressure processes. • Flexible operation. o Able to ramp up/down steam and electric power output. o Real-time power allocation for hydrogen production, electricity or both. • Multi-Module design for High Reliability. o Continuous power generation during refueling • Site boundary EPZ provides separation between Nuclear and Commercial Plant. o Industrial facility in proximity of a NuScale Small Modular Reactor (SMR) but outside of emergency planning zone. • Off-Grid dedicated power - Island Mode capability. o Does not require connection to the main transmission line. • Leverages proven Light Water Reactor Technology. o 67 years of operating history - existing supply chain. o Long-term, commercially available fuel. • Robust, Seismic Category I, Reactor Building with significant overpressure protection. • Only NRC certified Small Modular Reactor design 1.1 Brief Overview of the NuScale Technology The NuScale small modular reactor is based on proven light-water reactor technology with substantial improvement in nuclear safety. The innovative design is elegantly simple, incorporates extensive use of passive safety features, meets the utility needs for standardization while also allowing flexible deployment options, and is scalable to allow for incremental increases in electrical generating capacity. The robust design, small fuel inventory, and multiple barriers preventing fission product release contribute to a low probability and consequence of radionuclide release even under extreme upset conditions, thus simplifying the emergency preparedness and response, and providing a basis for reducing the emergency planning zone (EPZ). The NuScale SMR design consists of multiple NPMs, with each NPM representing an independent nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) coupled to a dedicated turbine-generator system. A flexible number Copyright © 2025 by NuScale Power LLC, Non-Proprietary WP-178373 Rev 1 Page 3 of 20 of NPMs can be configured into a power plant to suit an offtaker’s needs. A cutaway of the NPM is shown in Figure 1. It consists of a 250 MWt reactor core housed with other primary system components in an integral reactor pressure vessel surrounded by a steel containment vessel, all of which is immersed in a large pool of water that also serves as the ultimate heat sink. Above the core, there is a central hot riser tube, a helical coil steam generator surrounding the hot riser tube, and a pressurizer. The helical coil steam generator consists of two independent and interleaved sets of tube bundles with separate feedwater inlet and steam outlet lines. Sets of pressurizer heaters and spray nozzles located in the upper head of the vessel provide pressure control. Figure 1. NuScale Power Module Cutaway The primary reactor coolant path is upward through the reactor core. Heated water flows upward through the hot riser tube due to buoyancy forces and is turned downward at the pressurizer baffle plate. It then flows over the shell side of the steam generator, where it is cooled by conduction and convection of heat to the secondary coolant and continues to flow downward until its direction is again reversed at the lower reactor vessel head and turned upward back into the core. Coolant circulation is maintained entirely by natural buoyancy forces of the lower-density heated water exiting the core and the higher-density cooled water exiting the steam generator annulus. The reactor vessel is enclosed in a stainless-steel containment vessel (CNV) that is nominally 76 ft (23 m) tall and 15 ft (4.5 m) in diameter. The small-volume, high-design-pressure CNV is a unique feature of the NuScale design that contributes significantly to the large safety margins and overall resilience. The CNV has been designed as an ASME Class 1 pressure vessel. As a result, it can safely contain any loss-of-coolant-accident (LOCA) that can occur inside containment. During normal power operation, the CNV atmosphere is evacuated to provide an insulating vacuum that significantly reduces parasitic heat loss from the reactor vessel. Reactor building cutaway for a 6 NPM plant (462 MWe) 1.2 Designed for Electric Power and 100% Steam Bypass The NuScale SMR is the only commercial nuclear reactor design approved by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) for off-grid operation. The NuScale NPM is designed for 100% steam turbine bypass, either to the condenser or to an industrial end user. The NuScale design uses the Rankine thermal conversion cycle to produce electricity. In the secondary circuit of each NPM, feedwater is pumped into two feedwater headers of the steam generator, where it is heated by the primary coolant and boils to produce superheated steam. As shown in Figure 3, two main steam lines from each NPM combine into a single line and route the steam to a dedicated turbine-generator system that generates nominally 77 MWe (gross). Low pressure steam exiting the turbine is condensed and recirculated through three feedwater heater stages to the feedwater headers. The air-cooled condensers (wet cooled option shown below) are designed to accommodate 100% steam bypass of the turbine. The NPM is designed for load following using control rod motion and/or steam bypass. Thermal conversion system 1.3 Licensing The NuScale SMR is currently the only SMR that has received design certification from the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC). The NuScale Design Certification Application (DCA) was completed in December 2016. The review by the NRC commenced in March 2017. NuScale received standard design approval in September 2020 and design certification in January 2023 for its 50 MWe NPM configured as a 12-module plant. The DCA consisted of 12,000 pages of design information; requiring 2 million labor hours to prepare. It involved in excess of 50 suppliers and partners and approximately 800 people. The NRC review of the DCA was completed in 41 months, one month ahead of schedule. The review required an additional 2 million pages of supporting information and test data. Because of its unique safety, the NuScale design has received many “first-of-a-kind” approvals from the NRC, including: • No connection to the AC transmission grid required for safety. Regulations permits “off-grid” operation - A very important feature for providing reliable power and process heat to industrial applications. • NRC approved control room staffing. Three operators can safely operate 12 reactors in a single control room. • Elimination of Shift Technical Advisor (STA) position. • Use of unique cyber resistant Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) based Module Protection and Plant Protection Systems. • Approval of the NuScale Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ) sizing methodology. As opposed to a 10-mile radius EPZ, a site boundary EPZ is achievable for a power plant utilizing NPMs at most US sites surveyed. The NuScale design has two benefits regarding power output reliability. First, it permits staggered refueling so the total plant continues to produce 847 MWe (NuScale -12) or 385 MWe (NuScale - 6) gross, even during refueling. The second benefit, particularly for the NuScale-12, is the high degree of certainty of having 1 or 2 NPMs available to produce power. This is the range of power (77-154 MWe) typically needed for facilities that require ultra-high reliability for mission-critical processes. Operational reliability in a nuclear reactor depends on reactor scrams, which can cause shutdown. The NuScale reactor is designed to reduce the frequency of inadvertent reactor scrams compared to the currently operating reactor fleet. Energy resilience is the ability to prepare for and recover from energy disruptions. Resilient power systems minimize the effect of such costly failures. José Reyes and Daniel Ingersoll [3] assessed the energy resilience of a NuScale NPM relative to existing commercial nuclear fleet reactor scrams and noted that NuScale eliminated 70% of existing 2019 commercial nuclear fleet scrams by design. Three key factors can be attributed to the NuScale’s improved performance: (1) fewer and simpler systems, resulting in fewer potential failures, 2) island mode power, and (3) a 100% steam-turbine bypass [3]. Figure 4 shows the reduction is inadvertent shutdowns relative to commercial fleet. The expected capacity factor of a NuScale-based SMR power plant is ≥ 95%, which assumes a well-defined 10-day refueling outage for an 18-month fuel cycle and a conservatively assumed 3% forced outages. Longer fuel cycles are possible. Jeremiah Doyle’s peer reviewed paper [4] on mission-critical applications demonstrates that, because of its configuration as an independent unit and its island mode capability, a power plant utilizing 12 NPMs can provide highly reliable power to the grid. AMMONIA SYSTEMS Ammonia (NH3) production is the largest greenhouse gas emitter in the chemical industry. In 2020, 185 million metric tons of ammonia were produced, which generated 450 million metric tons of direct CO2 emissions, a ratio of 2.4 kg CO2 per 1.0 kg ammonia. This demand is expected to grow ~1% per year, for a projected demand of 230 million metric tons in 2050, even accounting for potential improvements in process efficiency and fertilizer application. Between 75 and 90% of this ammonia goes toward making fertilizer, and about 50% of the world’s food production relies on ammonia fertilizer. Ammonia is produced from the reaction of hydrogen (H2) and nitrogen (N2). More than 40% of global hydrogen production is used for ammonia production. For most existing ammonia plants, the hydrogen feed is produced on-site via the steam-methane reforming process, which uses a fossil fuel feedstock (natural gas or coal gasification). The hydrogen production process contributes to most (>90%) of the overall CO2 footprint for ammonia [8][9][10][11]. Coupling emissions-free electrical power and process heat from NPMs to an electrolyzer system will enable reliable large-scale decarbonized hydrogen production, independent of light or weather conditions.
